articles.title: Jaipur’s SMS Hospital opens dedicated heatwave ward amid rising temperatures - The News Mill
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Rajasthan
articles.full_text:
Hospitals in Rajasthan are establishing dedicated wards and forming specialised teams to manage health issues caused by the current severe heatwave across the state. Jaipur’s SMS Hospital, the largest government hospital and medical college in Rajasthan, has implemented special arrangements to treat patients affected by extreme heat conditions.
Dr Deepak Maheshwari, Principal and Controller of Jaipur SMS Hospital and Medical College, told ANI: “There are two things. First, regarding the arrangements we have made, when the heat is at its peak, and patients start coming in (though not many cases have arrived yet), we have identified a separate ward and a separate ICU for this. We have a nodal officer and a dedicated team for this purpose. So, we have made those arrangements. As of yesterday, I checked, and no such heatstroke cases have come in yet. So, I don’t think there will be any problem. We have designated an ICU.”
Several locations in North India are experiencing intense heatwave conditions. On Wednesday, Jaipur recorded a maximum temperature of 41 degrees Celsius. The World Meteorological Organization defines a heatwave as five or more consecutive days during which the daily maximum temperature exceeds the average maximum temperature by five degrees Celsius. On Wednesday, the temperature departure from the normal maximum was below two degrees Celsius in most places, except in Chittorgarh, which registered a deviation of at least 2.5 degrees.
Dr Maheshwari shared vital precautions for the public to avoid heat-related illnesses: “As for the general public, I consistently advise two or three things. First, those in extreme age groups, the more vulnerable ones like children and the elderly, should not go out in the sun. They should avoid being out when the temperature is at its peak. If they must go out, they should wear full-sleeved clothes, cover their bodies, and carry an umbrella for shade. Third, they must stay well-hydrated. Drink plenty of water, coconut water, or other fluids. If these three things are followed, I believe 70% of cases are preventable.”
He added, “The elderly, children, and those who are weaker, meaning the elderly, children, and people with multiple underlying diseases or those who are already unwell, need to be particularly careful. Others should also take precautions, but these groups are more vulnerable and may face more difficulties.”
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast that heatwave conditions will worsen in parts of northwest India, with rising temperatures expected in West Rajasthan in the coming days. Additionally, hot and humid weather will prevail across several coastal areas, with isolated warm night conditions predicted in central India.
The IMD noted that heatwave conditions are very likely in isolated parts of Vidarbha on April 29, followed by a prolonged spell over West Rajasthan from May 1 to May 3.
